agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"An existing production logistics system enforces a strict 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier relationship with supervisor-gated reassignment. The product team requests an 'auto-split' feature that would automatically decompose overweight shipments (>500 kg) into sub-shipments, each assigned to a different carrier, without supervisor approval.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of freight that must have exactly one assigned Carrier. The atomic unit of dispatch."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The party responsible for physically transporting a Shipment."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The locked binding between a Shipment and its single Carrier. Immutable once set, except by Supervisor override."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"The only role authorized to reassign a Carrier on an existing Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-Shipment","definition":"A concept NOT present in the current domain model. The feature request introduces this term without defining its relationship to the existing Shipment aggregate."},{"term":"Auto-Split","definition":"Proposed automatic decomposition of a single Shipment into multiple Sub-Shipments when weight exceeds 500 kg. This concept does not exist in the current model."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a core aggregate invariant enforcing data consistency. A Shipment without exactly one Carrier is in a corrupt state — billing, tracking, liability, and dispatch all depend on this 1:1 relationship.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in any non-draft state, COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign it.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization invariant protecting state integrity. Without it, carrier assignments could be silently changed, breaking chain-of-custody, audit trails, and contractual obligations.","testable_assertion":"Any Carrier reassignment event must have an associated Supervisor identity. System-initiated reassignments without Supervisor approval must be rejected."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment has exactly one Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Dispatch is a physical real-world event. A shipment cannot leave a facility without a single accountable carrier. This is a physical constraint, not a policy.","testable_assertion":"The dispatch operation must fail if the Shipment's assigned Carrier count != 1."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A shipment weighing exactly 500 kg — does it split or not?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The request says 'exceeds 500 kg' but boundary behavior must be specified precisely (> vs >=)."},{"scenario":"A shipment is auto-split, then one sub-shipment's carrier becomes unavailable — who has authority to reassign?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The auto-split bypasses supervisor involvement, but the existing model requires a supervisor for any reassignment."},{"scenario":"A sub-shipment itself exceeds 500 kg after splitting — does it recursively split?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. No recursion or minimum sub-shipment weight is specified."},{"scenario":"The original shipment has already been assigned a carrier before the split is triggered — what happens to that locked assignment?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Breaking the existing carrier lock without supervisor approval directly violates BR-2."},{"scenario":"Liability dispute: goods are damaged in transit across one of multiple sub-shipments — which carrier is liable and under which contract?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The 1:1 Shipment-Carrier model currently makes liability unambiguous. Multi-carrier sub-shipments fracture this."},{"scenario":"Customer tracking: the customer shipped one shipment but now sees multiple sub-shipments — how is this presented?","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The customer-facing domain concept of a 'Shipment' is fractured without a parent-child relationship being defined."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk. Carrier contracts, billing, and insurance are structured around the 1:1 Shipment-Carrier model. Auto-splitting without redefining these relationships could create unbilled freight, disputed invoices, and liability gaps.","operational":"Significant complexity increase. Dispatch, tracking, proof-of-delivery, and exception handling all assume one carrier per shipment. Every downstream process must be audited and potentially redesigned.","user":"Customer confusion is likely. A customer who booked one shipment now sees multiple sub-shipments with different carriers and potentially different delivery times. Without a unifying 'Order' or 'Consignment' concept, the UX degrades.","risk":"CRITICAL. This feature as specified directly violates two production invariants (BR-1's spirit of single accountability, and BR-2's supervisor-gated reassignment). Deploying it without resolving these contradictions risks data corruption, audit failures, and contractual breaches.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Must handle multi-carrier coordination for what was previously a single-carrier shipment. Increases dispatch complexity and error surface."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Billing logic assumes 1 shipment = 1 carrier invoice. Sub-shipments require a new billing model or reconciliation layer."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their authority over carrier assignment is being bypassed for a class of shipments. This undermines the control structure they rely on."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Carriers now receive partial shipments. Their contracts, SLAs, and pricing models may not account for sub-shipment handling."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Tracking, delivery expectations, and dispute resolution all become more complex when one shipment becomes many."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Chain-of-custody, liability assignment, and insurance coverage are all structured around 1:1 Shipment-Carrier. Splitting without legal review creates exposure."}]},"confidence":{"level":40,"reasoning":"The feature request directly contradicts two established production invariants. Until the product team clarifies whether they intend to (a) redefine the Shipment aggregate, (b) introduce a new Parent Shipment / Sub-Shipment hierarchy, or (c) relax existing invariants, no acceptance criteria can be safely defined. The spec as written is internally contradictory with the existing domain model.","high_confidence_areas":["The contradiction between auto-split and BR-1/BR-2 is unambiguous","The edge cases around liability, billing, and tracking are real operational risks"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends to redefine the Shipment aggregate or introduce a new concept","Whether the 500 kg threshold is a hard business rule or a configurable policy","Whether 'without supervisor involvement' means no human oversight at all or just no manual trigger"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model as described is accurate and enforced in production","The product team has not yet defined the relationship between a parent shipment and its sub-shipments","No new aggregate (e.g., Consignment, Order) has been proposed to wrap the sub-shipments"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature requires multiple carriers per shipment, directly violating BR-1 (exactly one carrier per shipment) and BR-2 (supervisor-only reassignment). The feature cannot be implemented as described without breaking production invariants.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Finance","Supervisors","Carriers","Customers","Legal"],"options":["A: Introduce a new aggregate (e.g., 'Consignment') that owns multiple Shipments, preserving the 1:1 Shipment-Carrier invariant. The split creates new Shipments under a parent Consignment.","B: Redefine the Shipment aggregate to support a parent-child relationship where the parent is a logical shipment and children are physical shipments, each with one carrier.","C: Relax BR-1 and BR-2 to allow multi-carrier shipments — this is the most dangerous option as it invalidates all downstream systems built on the current invariants.","D: Reject the feature as incompatible with the domain model and propose an alternative (e.g., manual split with supervisor approval)."],"recommendation":"Option A. Introduce a 'Consignment' aggregate that represents the customer's original intent (one logical shipment). The auto-split creates multiple Shipments under this Consignment, each preserving the 1:1 Carrier invariant. Supervisor bypass can be scoped narrowly to the initial system-generated split event only, with a new 'SystemSplit' event type that is auditable. This preserves all existing invariants while enabling the business capability."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to what the domain currently models as a single Shipment","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier"},{"violation":"Auto-split bypasses supervisor approval for carrier assignment on split sub-shipments","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on an existing Shipment"}]}
